text: Four laparoscopic ports are visible, with one port placed at an old prostatectomy scar for specimen extraction. An additional port near the upper right trochanter is optional.

During the abdominal exploration, the surgeon observes the absence of ascites and carcinosis. The preoperative tattoo is visible on the proximal transverse colon, and a ten-centimeter distal margin is marked with a hemlock clip.

The surgeon begins the dissection at the duodenum, employing a bottom-up lateral-to-medial approach. The dissection carefully progresses around the D-II segment and the head of the pancreas, with the surgeon positioned between the patient's legs.

The surgeon opens the right parietocolic gutter using an ultrasonic device, extending the dissection up to the liver. The hepatic flexure is then mobilized, with the surgeon now positioned on the left side of the patient.
The surgeon ligates and sections the ileocolic and right branch of the middle colic vessels, followed by transection of the mesocolon and mesentery using an ultrasonic device.

The surgeon completes the right hemicolectomy by transecting the colon and terminal ileum using a sixty linear stapler, introduced through the right lower trochanter. The distal and proximal margins have been dissected, and the specimen is ready for removal.

The specimen is removed through a protected suprapubic incision. The pneumoperitoneum is restored, and the ileum and colon stumps are approximated in an antiperistaltic fashion without traction. The medial ends of both staple lines are excised, and a linear stapler is inserted through the anterotomies via the right lower trocar and fired. The surgeon stands between the legs, with the assistant on the left side of the patient.

A stay suture is placed to provide traction on the posterior and anterior walls of the anastomosis. The enterotomy is then closed using a sixty blue cartridge stapler, inserted through the left lower trocar.

The surgeon approximates the colon and ileum without dividing them, performing two enterotomies at the future anastomosis site. The stapler is inserted and fired, completing the anastomosis and resection simultaneously. The surgeon remains positioned between the legs, using the right lower trocar.

The surgeon closes the enterotomy transversely using a stapler, simultaneously resecting the proximal and distal margins. The surgical field is checked to ensure no mesenteric interposition posteriorly, and the length of the anastomosis is carefully evaluated.

The final result of the ileocolic resection is shown, with the anastomosis completed using four crotch stitches to prevent traction on the distal end. The mesentery appears untwisted, and the surgical site is stable.
